topic: prompt&score ~ public /mech interp on cheatsheet effect

messages.content:
Hi everyone! Newbie here. My day-work is on AI assisted hw formal verification, my research interests are on mechanistic interpretability and my pasttime obsession is on equivalence mapping between different representation formats; these merged a bit after playing in the competition's playground yesterday.

I found that this challenge raises an interesting question: when a cheatsheet improves a model's accuracy, does it change what the model represents internally, or how it routes representations to output? More so, mechanistic interpretations on tasks like verified equivalence checking is specifically interesting to explore.

Will the submitted cheatsheets &their score(s) be made public or made available for collaborations? I'd love to explore this further.
messages.subject: prompt&score ~ public /mech interp on cheatsheet effect

messages.content:
Luiza Corpaci said:

Hi everyone! Newbie here. My day-work is on AI assisted hw formal verification, my research interests are on mechanistic interpretability and my pasttime obsession is on equivalence mapping between different representation formats; these merged a bit after playing in the competition's playground yesterday.

I found that this challenge raises an interesting question: when a cheatsheet improves a model's accuracy, does it change what the model represents internally, or how it routes representations to output? More so, mechanistic interpretations on tasks like verified equivalence checking is specifically interesting to explore.

Will the submitted cheatsheets &their score(s) be made public or made available for collaborations? I'd love to explore this further.

Welcome! Your background in AI formal verification + mechanistic interpretability + equivalence mapping is a perfect match for this challenge, I can see why the playground got you hooked.

Great question: cheatsheets can both shift what the model represents internally and how it routes/combines things for the output. Prompts definitely can tweak internal dynamics, and probing that with equivalence checking sounds promising.

I think organisers are planning to share cheatsheets, crediting authors and forming optimum collaborations. Would love to see any interp work you run on this!
messages.subject: prompt&score ~ public /mech interp on cheatsheet effect

messages.content:
Luiza Corpaci said:

Hi everyone! Newbie here. My day-work is on AI assisted hw formal verification, my research interests are on mechanistic interpretability and my pasttime obsession is on equivalence mapping between different representation formats; these merged a bit after playing in the competition's playground yesterday.

I found that this challenge raises an interesting question: when a cheatsheet improves a model's accuracy, does it change what the model represents internally, or how it routes representations to output? More so, mechanistic interpretations on tasks like verified equivalence checking is specifically interesting to explore.

Will the submitted cheatsheets &their score(s) be made public or made available for collaborations? I'd love to explore this further.

hi, i am of similar interests (mech interp). I've saturated hard1 and hard2 benchmarks, and my intuition is (still a pure speculation on my side, i didn't back this up with anything tangible) - as you for sure know, there probably is the equivalent of gradient descent happening in context. What I would bet is happening - even smallest models are capable enough that given the correct in context guidelines, they "learn" how to better answer these equations. Or, another idea is, that these cheatsheets activate the dormant places inside their latent space that a naive "solve this equation" simply isn't reaching successfully, and a more rich representation is needed. But one would need to trace activations (which I think wouldn't be hard at all, since you can run these locally - I was able to get near 80% on hard2 with llama 8b!).

in some cases, i think you are right about changing the way model tries to solve these too. for example, i'd bet they attempt to solve it algebraically, while the best benchmark saturation method for GPT OSS models (both 120b and 20b) was to treat these as general solvers.

if you thought about some serious mech interp work for this challenge, i'd be interested. Since I am curious what is really happening in models.
messages.subject: prompt&score ~ public /mech interp on cheatsheet effect
